pr.title: Add motion index comments and counts
pr.author: ribbanya

summary: Annotated fighter motion-state tables with explicit enum/index comments and converted many previously unsized MotionState arrays to compile-time-sized arrays using per-character SelfCount constants. The PR also added or extended forward declarations for motion/submotion Count and SelfCount enums, created ftdata3.h, and exposed ftData_MotionStateList as sized by ftCo_MS_Count. There was no PR body or review discussion in the slice, so intent is inferred from the title and diff: make motion table lengths and index alignment searchable and mechanically checkable without altering table contents.
what_changed: Changed many character-specific MotionState table definitions from unsized arrays such as MotionState ftCa_Init_MotionStateTable[] to sized arrays such as MotionState ftCa_Init_MotionStateTable[ftCa_MS_SelfCount].
Updated matching extern declarations in character Init headers so table declarations and definitions use the same [*_MS_SelfCount] bound.
Added inline comments before motion-state table entries recording the intended motion enum and absolute index, e.g. // ftCa_MS_SwordSwing4 = 341 and // ftLk_MS_AttackS42 = 341.

Added Count and SelfCount enum constants to many character forward headers, following the pattern *_MS_Count and *_MS_SelfCount = *_MS_Count - ftCo_MS_Count, with analogous *_SM_Count and *_SM_SelfCount for submotions.
Added src/melee/ft/chara/ftCrazyHand/forward.h and defined Crazy Hand counts in terms of Master Hand counts: ftCh_MS_Count = ftMh_MS_Count - 1 and ftCh_MS_SelfCount = ftMh_MS_SelfCount - 1.

Sized clone/shared-character motion tables by the source character's count where appropriate, such as ftCl_Init_MotionStateTable[ftLk_MS_SelfCount], ftDr_Init_MotionStateTable[ftMr_MS_SelfCount], ftFe_Init_MotionStateTable[ftMs_MS_SelfCount], ftFc_Init_MotionStateTable[ftFx_MS_SelfCount], ftGn_Init_MotionStateTable[ftCa_MS_SelfCount], ftGk_Init_MotionStateTable[ftKp_MS_SelfCount], ftNn_Init_MotionStateTable[ftPp_MS_SelfCount], and ftPc_Init_MotionStateTable[ftPk_MS_SelfCount].

Created ftdata3.h with an extern declaration for ftData_MotionStateList[ftCo_MS_Count] and changed ftdata3.c to include ftCommon/forward.h and define ftData_MotionStateList with that common motion-state count.
smart_moves: Using SelfCount constants turns table length assumptions into type-level information, making future omissions or extra entries easier for the compiler and reviewers to spot.
Keeping absolute index comments next to each initializer preserves the decompilation-relevant mapping between enum names, table order, and in-game motion IDs without changing generated data layout.

Deriving SelfCount as Count minus ftCo_MS_Count documents the split between common fighter states and character-specific states, which is important because character tables begin after common motion state 340 and entries in the excerpt start at 341.
For clone or shared moveset characters, reusing the parent/source count avoids duplicating parallel enums when the table is intentionally aligned to another character's motion enum namespace.
Adding the required forward-header includes to Init headers keeps the new sized extern declarations self-contained rather than relying on include order elsewhere.
Adding ftdata3.h gives the common motion-state table a proper typed declaration instead of leaving ftData_MotionStateList as an unsized local extern pattern.
decomp_lessons: For motion-state tables, do not leave arrays unsized when an enum count exists or can be introduced; use [*_MS_SelfCount] for character-specific tables and [ftCo_MS_Count] for the common table.
When adding Count and SelfCount to fighter enums, mirror the pattern for both motion states and submotions: *_MS_Count, *_MS_SelfCount, *_SM_Count, *_SM_SelfCount.
Index comments are valuable decomp scaffolding: place comments immediately above table entries so enum order, raw numeric index, and initializer contents can be audited together.

The character-specific motion table indices are relative to the self table but correspond to absolute motion-state IDs beginning after ftCo_MS_Count; comments such as '= 341' make that relationship explicit.

Clone characters may legitimately size their table with another fighter's SelfCount when their motion table uses that fighter's motion enum symbols; do not force a new independent count without evidence.
Header declarations must include the forward header that defines the count expression used in an array bound, or downstream users become include-order dependent.
naming_conventions: SelfCount expression pattern: *_MS_SelfCount = *_MS_Count - ftCo_MS_Count and *_SM_SelfCount = *_SM_Count - ftCo_SM_Count.
Motion index comment pattern: // <motion_state_enum> = <absolute_index>, placed directly before the entry initializer.

Shared/clone count reuse examples include ftLk_MS_SelfCount for Young Link, ftMr_MS_SelfCount for Dr. Mario, ftFx_MS_SelfCount for Falco, ftCa_MS_SelfCount for Ganon, ftKp_MS_SelfCount for Giga Koopa, ftPp_MS_SelfCount for Nana, and ftPk_MS_SelfCount for Pichu.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: This PR appears primarily non-behavioral: it adds comments, enum count constants, includes, and array bounds without evidence of function logic changes.
Sizing arrays by enum counts is a low-risk matching tactic because it should preserve initializer order and data contents while increasing compile-time validation of table length.
Absolute motion index comments help future matching work correlate raw numeric motion IDs observed in assembly or data references with enum names and table offsets.
Introducing ftdata3.h and declaring ftData_MotionStateList[ftCo_MS_Count] makes the common motion-state table easier to reference consistently during later data/function matching.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rely on *_MS_SelfCount or *_SM_SelfCount for fighter table matching.
Audit clone character motion-state tables that are sized by another character's SelfCount to confirm the sharing remains intentional.
Search for remaining unsized MotionState arrays after PR 819.
Check whether motion index comments were later converted into designated initializers or generated enum/table validation.
